title: Certifying Uniface Applications
html:
Certifying Uniface Applications

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication

Save selected topicSave selected topic and subtopicsSave entire publication

Email LinkCopy Link
Feedback
  • Last UpdatedMärz 14, 2025
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

Certifying a deployed Uniface application can safeguard the privacy of your data. This functionality helps you to verify the validity of your application during runtime, making sure that your database connection settings cannot be used with unauthorized applications.

To achieve this, Uniface allows "Certifying" (similar to Digital Signing) to verify that your database connection can only be used with your own authorized Uniface applications, and that your application has not been tampered with.

Certifying and Cryptography

Publishing certified binaries, similar to Digitally Signed binaries, is a common practice among software vendors, which helps verify the validity of the deployed application.

How it works:

  • A private/public key pair must be generated, for example my_private.pem and my_public.pem.
  • The private key my_private.pem will be used to certify a deployed application.
  • The public key my_public.pem will be linked with your database connection.
  • A database connection linked with my_public.pem must match a deployed application certified with my_private.pem.
  • Malicious applications that are signed with foreign private keys will be unable to use your database connection.

Why is it important:

When Uniface applications are certified, Uniface validates the integrity of every supported resource file. Any malicious attempts to modify the Uniface application is mitigated.

Additionally, assignment files contain sensitive information about your database connection. When certifying your application, you are certain that only authorized Uniface applications can use your database connection.

Specifications:

  • Private keys:
    • The private key is stored in PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) format.
    • The private key is generated using an Auto-Seeded Random generator and a size of 2048 bits.
    • The algorithm used is RSA and the format is PKCS#8, in compliance with RFC5208.
    • Private keys cannot exceed 4096 characters.
  • Public keys:
    • The public key is stored in PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) format.
    • When using cert.exe, the public key is derived from the generated private key.
    • When using OpenSSL, multiple public keys can be derived from the same private key.
    • Public keys cannot exceed 1024 characters.

Was this topic helpful?
